title: 10 Best Restaurants in Pattaya 2026: Seafood from ฿200 & Local Gems
meta_description: Pattaya locals eat here: Nang Nual seafood ฿300, Fra Pattaya duck ฿150, Breezeo sunset views. No tourist traps — real food, real prices, real Pattaya.
intro: Pattaya's dining scene runs deeper than its beach-resort reputation suggests. Between the go-go bars and beach clubs, you'll find a city that has been feeding fishermen, expats, and Thai families for generations. Nang Nual has been grilling seafood on Walking Street since 1965. Fra Pattaya serves lacquered duck in a local market setting that most tourists walk straight past. The Royal Cliff's Breezeo catches the Gulf of Thailand sunset over plates of fresh fish. These are real restaurants with real histories — not names dreamed up for a listicle. Whether you want a ฿50 bowl of soup on Soi 15 or a ฿600 seafood spread overlooking the bay, Pattaya delivers if you know where to look.

items.name: Nang Nual Restaurant
items.story: One of Pattaya's oldest and most respected seafood restaurants, Nang Nual has operated on Walking Street since 1965 — long before the street became synonymous with nightlife. The extensive menu covers fresh seafood alongside Thai, Western, Italian, Chinese, and Japanese dishes, but the seafood is the reason regulars return: whole grilled fish, steamed crab, and platters of tiger prawns laid out on ice at the entrance so you can choose your catch before it goes to the kitchen.
items.why_locals_love_it: Six decades in business is the answer. Pattaya residents trust Nang Nual for family celebrations and special occasions because the kitchen has never had to reinvent itself — the quality has been consistent long enough to outlast entire generations of competitors. The wine cellar and waterfront setting make it feel like an occasion without requiring a Bangkok price tag.
insider_tips: Point at the fresh seafood display at the entrance and agree on a price per kilogram before ordering — it avoids surprises on the bill.
The restaurant has a wine cellar, so a proper bottle with fresh crab is entirely possible here.
Book a table on the waterfront side for the evening breeze and views over the bay.
items.price_reality: Seafood priced by weight, typically ฿250–600 per dish depending on the catch. A full seafood dinner for two runs ฿600–1,200 including drinks.
items.location_details: 214 Moo 10 Walking Street, South Pattaya. At the southern end of Walking Street, waterfront side.
items.personal_moment: Sitting at a waterfront table at dusk, ordering steamed crab and a cold Singha while the fishing boats come back into the bay — this is the Pattaya that existed before the neon signs went up.

items.name: Fra Pattaya
items.story: Fra Pattaya is a Sino-Thai restaurant on South Pattaya Road that draws a steady local crowd for its lacquered duck — the dish the place is known for. The setting is unfussy, closer to a neighbourhood canteen than a tourist restaurant, which is exactly why the food stays honest. The Chinese-Thai cooking tradition here reflects the community of Thai-Chinese families who have shaped Pattaya's food culture since the city's fishing village days.
items.why_locals_love_it: Thai-Chinese families in Pattaya gravitate to Fra Pattaya for duck rice and braised pork in the same way Bangkok residents line up at their neighbourhood duck shop. The prices stay affordable, the portions are generous, and it doesn't pander to tourist expectations.
insider_tips: Order the lacquered duck over rice as the main — that is the dish the restaurant is built around.
Arrive during the lunch rush (noon–1pm) when the duck comes fresh off the hook.
The restaurant sits in a local market-style area; park on South Pattaya Road and walk in.
items.price_reality: Duck rice dishes ฿80–150. A full meal with soup and drinks for two stays well under ฿400.
items.location_details: 597/9 South Pattaya Road (S Pattaya Rd), central Pattaya.
items.personal_moment: A plate of lacquered duck over rice at a formica table, surrounded by Thai-Chinese regulars who've been ordering the same thing for years — this is the Pattaya that doesn't show up on Instagram.

items.name: Breezeo at Royal Cliff Beach Hotel
items.story: Breezeo occupies a privileged position at the Royal Cliff Beach Hotel complex on the southern headland above Pattaya Bay, giving it one of the best sunset vantage points along this stretch of the Gulf of Thailand. The menu focuses on Thai food and fresh seafood with indoor and outdoor terrace seating. It's the kind of place where the setting does half the work — fresh fish and a view of the bay as the light turns orange is a combination that's hard to fault.
items.why_locals_love_it: Pattaya residents who want a proper dinner with atmosphere come to Breezeo for anniversaries and family gatherings. The Royal Cliff is one of Pattaya's established luxury hotels, and Breezeo carries that reputation without demanding Bangkok fine-dining prices.
insider_tips: Request a terrace table when booking — the outdoor seats with bay views are the reason to come here.
Visit between 5:30pm and 7pm to catch the sunset over the Gulf of Thailand.
The Thai set menus offer better value than ordering à la carte if you want to try multiple dishes.
items.price_reality: Thai mains ฿180–450, seafood dishes ฿300–700. Dinner for two with drinks runs ฿800–1,600.
items.location_details: Royal Cliff Beach Hotel & Royal Cliff Beach Terrace, Pratamnak Hill, South Pattaya. Open 11:00–23:00.
items.personal_moment: Watching the sun sink into the Gulf from a terrace table, cold drink in hand, with a plate of grilled fish between you and whoever you brought — Pattaya earns its reputation in moments like this.

items.name: Five Star J. Restaurant
items.story: Five Star J. is Pattaya's best-known fully vegetarian restaurant, offering a 100% lacto-vegetarian menu with vegan options available. It sits at the junction of South Pattaya Road and Third Road, a busy corner that keeps it well-supplied with the Thai Buddhist community and the growing number of travellers who want a break from seafood and meat. The menu covers Thai vegetarian staples done properly — not the sad salad-bar approach of tourist restaurants but genuine Thai cooking with plant-based proteins.
items.why_locals_love_it: Thai Buddhists observing meatless eating periods (especially during the Vegetarian Festival in October) have long relied on Five Star J. The restaurant caters to a real community need, not a trend, which keeps the kitchen focused and the prices reasonable.
insider_tips: Visit during the Thai Vegetarian Festival (October) when the full menu comes into its own and the restaurant is packed with locals.
The lunch set menus (before 2pm) offer three or four dishes with rice for well under ฿150.
Ask which dishes are fully vegan if dairy avoidance matters — staff are familiar with the question.
items.price_reality: Main dishes ฿60–150. A full meal with drinks rarely exceeds ฿250 per person.
items.location_details: 313/37 South Pattaya Road at the junction with Third Road, South Pattaya. Open 09:00–21:00.
items.personal_moment: A quiet lunch here during the week — a table of Thai monks at one end, a group of Western vegetarian travellers at the other, everyone eating the same honest food at the same honest prices.

items.name: Moom Talay Thai Restaurant (at Trattoria Pizzeria Toscana)
items.story: This is one of Pattaya's more interesting dining addresses: an Italian trattoria and a Thai restaurant sharing the same beachfront building between Sois 6 and 7 on Beach Road. The Italian side bakes its own bread and makes its own pasta; the Thai side runs a proper kitchen serving central Thai dishes. For visitors who can't decide between pad thai and pizza, or for groups with mixed appetites, the arrangement works surprisingly well. The beachfront location on Beach Road means you eat within metres of the Gulf.
items.why_locals_love_it: The combined format suits Pattaya's mixed local-expat demographic. Thai families can bring foreign partners or colleagues without anyone compromising. The bread and pasta being made in-house rather than shipped in frozen is noticed and appreciated by the expat regulars.
insider_tips: The Italian side's homemade pasta is the draw — avoid the generic pizzas that most beach restaurants do better elsewhere.
On the Thai side, order the seafood dishes: the beachfront location means the supply chain is short.
Thai dishes cost around ฿180, pizzas around ฿300 — budget accordingly before you sit down.
items.price_reality: Thai dishes ฿150–300, Italian pasta/pizza ฿250–400. German beer on tap at ฿200 for half a litre.
items.location_details: 98/4 Moo 9 Beach Road, between Soi 6 and Soi 7, central Pattaya beachfront.
items.personal_moment: Sitting on the beach side at lunch, eating homemade tagliatelle while the beach vendors work the sand twenty metres away — an odd combination that only makes sense in Pattaya.

items.name: PIC Kitchen
items.story: PIC Kitchen on Soi 5 (Pattaya 5 Road) is one of the city's most recommended traditional Thai restaurants, occupying a converted Thai house with a garden setting that feels far removed from the Beach Road bustle. The menu covers the full range of central and royal Thai cuisine — dishes that require skill and time to prepare, not the abbreviated tourist versions. Locals and long-term expats point to PIC Kitchen when visitors ask where to eat proper Thai food in an actual Thai setting.
items.why_locals_love_it: The garden house setting gives it an atmosphere that beach-strip restaurants can't replicate, and the cooking backs it up. Thai families bring visiting relatives here precisely because it represents Thai food and hospitality done correctly — the kind of meal you'd be proud to show someone who has never eaten in Thailand.
insider_tips: Book ahead for dinner, especially on weekends — the garden tables fill up with Thai and expat regulars.
Order a mix of dishes to share rather than individual plates; the food is designed for group eating.
The set menus for two or four people offer good value and introduce the range of the kitchen.
items.price_reality: Main dishes ฿180–420. A proper multi-dish dinner for two runs ฿600–1,000 including drinks.
items.location_details: Soi 5 (Pattaya 5 Road), Central Pattaya. Look for the traditional Thai house set back from the road.
items.personal_moment: Eating tom kha gai and green curry in a lantern-lit garden while cicadas make their noise in the trees overhead — this is what Thai restaurant ambience is supposed to feel like.

items.name: Ruen Thai Restaurant
items.story: Ruen Thai is a long-established garden restaurant in Pattaya that serves traditional Thai cuisine in an open-air setting designed around Thai architectural elements — pavilions, tropical plants, and wooden structures that create a sense of dining somewhere genuinely Thai rather than a beach-resort approximation of it. The restaurant has been recommended in travel guides to Pattaya for many years and maintains a reputation for consistent cooking and gracious service.
items.why_locals_love_it: Ruen Thai occupies a similar niche to PIC Kitchen: a destination for when the occasion calls for something that feels like proper Thailand. Thai families celebrating birthdays or welcoming foreign guests choose it because the setting does the talking before the food even arrives.
insider_tips: Evening visits are better than lunch — the garden atmosphere comes into its own after dark with the lighting.
Order the miang kham (leaf-wrapped appetiser) to start: it is a dish that shows what the kitchen can do with traditional preparation.
The restaurant can accommodate large groups with advance notice, making it useful for family dinners.
items.price_reality: Starters ฿100–200, main dishes ฿180–380. A full dinner for two with drinks ฿600–1,000.
items.location_details: South Pattaya area. Confirm current address before visiting as the restaurant has relocated previously.
items.personal_moment: Sitting in a wooden pavilion surrounded by tropical garden, eating a proper southern Thai curry with fresh roti — the kind of meal that makes you wonder why you ate at the hotel buffet the night before.

items.name: Mantra Restaurant & Bar
items.story: Mantra at the Amari Ocean Hotel is one of Pattaya's most ambitious restaurant concepts: a single dining room divided into eight cuisine stations covering Thai, Indian, Chinese, Japanese, Mediterranean, and European cooking, all visible from an open kitchen. The design — dark wood, dramatic lighting, open show kitchens — makes it one of the more striking dining rooms in the city. It pulls a mixed crowd of hotel guests, Pattaya expats, and Bangkok weekenders who want dinner to be an event.
items.why_locals_love_it: For a special occasion dinner in Pattaya, Mantra delivers on atmosphere in a way that few restaurants in the city can match. The variety of cuisine stations means large groups with different tastes can all find something, which makes it practical for corporate dinners and celebrations where pleasing everyone matters.
insider_tips: The tasting menu is the best way to experience the range of the kitchen — ordering à la carte from eight different cuisines can feel overwhelming.
Book the bar seating near the open kitchen if you want to watch the chefs work.
Happy hour at the bar before dinner keeps the overall bill manageable.
items.price_reality: Mains ฿350–800, set menus ฿800–1,500 per person. One of Pattaya's pricier dinner options, justified by the setting and execution.
items.location_details: Amari Ocean Hotel, 240 Moo 5 Beach Road, North Pattaya.
items.personal_moment: Watching a teppanyaki chef work the flat iron while a Thai curry arrives from the station across the room — Mantra is theatrical in the best sense, and the food matches the staging.

items.name: Casa Pascal
items.story: Casa Pascal is a Pattaya institution run by Swiss chef Pascal Schnyder, combining European fine-dining technique with Thai ingredients and hospitality. The restaurant has operated in Pattaya for many years and built a loyal following among long-term expats and Thai professional families who want European cooking done properly — not the watered-down version that most beach-town restaurants offer. The menu moves between classic European preparations and dishes that fold in Thai produce and flavour profiles.
items.why_locals_love_it: Pattaya's substantial Swiss, German, and Scandinavian expat community has adopted Casa Pascal as the place where European standards are actually maintained. The chef's involvement in the kitchen rather than delegating to untrained staff is what keeps quality consistent — a rarity in a city where restaurant turnover is high.
insider_tips: The weekend brunch is popular with expat families — book ahead to get a table.
Ask about the chef's daily specials, which use whatever is freshest from Thai markets that morning.
The wine list is more considered than you'd expect from a Pattaya restaurant — worth a look before defaulting to beer.
items.price_reality: European mains ฿450–900, set menus ฿700–1,200. One of the city's more expensive options, but the standard reflects the price.
items.location_details: Pattaya — confirm current address before visiting. Previously located near Second Road, Central Pattaya.
items.personal_moment: A proper European dinner in tropical heat, with a Thai twist in the seasoning and Thai warmth in the service — Casa Pascal threads that needle better than any other restaurant in the city.

items.name: Sailor Bar and Restaurant
items.story: Sailor sits 30 metres up Soi 8 from Beach Road — close enough to the beach to catch the breeze, far enough back to keep the prices honest. The menu mixes Thai dishes with European standbys and the kitchen opens early, making it one of the few places in central Pattaya serving a proper cooked breakfast for ฿65. It's a working expat canteen as much as a restaurant: the kind of place where regulars read the newspaper at the same table every morning and the staff know their order before they sit down.
items.why_locals_love_it: Pattaya's expat community values Sailor for reliability and price. In a city where restaurant costs can escalate quickly the moment you step onto Beach Road, Sailor stays predictable. The ฿65 breakfast in particular has become something of a local benchmark for value.
insider_tips: The ฿65 breakfast (eggs, toast, coffee) is the best-value meal in central Pattaya — come before 9am to avoid the post-beach crowd.
The Thai dishes at lunch and dinner are a notch more authentic than the Western options, worth ordering if you're not committed to eggs and toast.
The Soi 8 location puts you one block from the beach but away from the Beach Road price premium.
items.price_reality: Breakfast sets ฿65–120, Thai mains ฿80–180, European dishes ฿150–280. One of central Pattaya's most affordable sit-down options.
items.location_details: Soi 8 (approximately 30 metres from Beach Road junction), Central Pattaya Beach area.
items.personal_moment: A ฿65 breakfast with strong coffee and a newspaper while the beach vendors set up their chairs outside — the quiet hour before Pattaya wakes up is its own reward.

local_wisdom: Many locals prefer to eat at home or at family-run places, so seek out small, unpretentious eateries for authentic food.
Don't be afraid to try street food; some of the best meals are served from food carts.
Always greet the staff with a smile; it goes a long way in Thailand!
budget_hacks: Look for lunch specials; many restaurants offer discounted meals during midday.
Sharing dishes with friends can help you try more without breaking the bank.
Street food is not only affordable but also an adventure in flavors!
cultural_notes: When dining in Thailand, it's common to share dishes, so don't be surprised if your table is filled with plates. Also, always show respect to the staff by using 'khob khun ka/krap' (thank you) when you leave.
perplexity_query: Top 10 best restaurants in Pattaya, Thailand in 2025. Include for each: current prices in Thai Baht, opening hours, exact location/address, recent reviews or ratings, specialties/signature dishes, any new openings in 2024-2025. Focus on authentic local favorites and highly rated establishments.
